restatement: Build a single-page web application that lets a user play the card game blackjack against a computer dealer in their browser. The user prompt names blackjack as the game and 'web app' as the form factor; everything else (rules variant, visual style, persistence, multiplayer, accounts, money) is unspecified and will default to the simplest standalone single-player experience that demonstrably plays blackjack end-to-end.

assumption_ledger.assumption: Single human player versus a computer dealer; no multiplayer, no other seats at the table.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Prompt says 'play blackjack' (singular) with no mention of opponents, friends, or other players. Casino blackjack is canonically player-vs-dealer; adding multiplayer would be extra scope the prompt never asked for.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If the user actually wanted multiplayer, the entire turn-management, networking, and state-sync layer would be missing — a near-total rebuild.

assumption_ledger.assumption: Runs entirely client-side in the browser with no backend server, no database, and no API calls.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Blackjack requires no server-side logic to play locally. Prompt does not mention accounts, leaderboards, or persistence. A static front-end is the simplest defensible interpretation of 'web app' for this game.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If persistence, accounts, or anti-cheat were required, the architecture would need a server, database, and auth system — none of which are in scope here.

assumption_ledger.assumption: No user accounts, login, or authentication of any kind. Anyone who opens the page can play.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Prompt is silent on identity. Auth is a heavy addition that 'play blackjack' does not imply. Default to anonymous, open access.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: Per-user history, friend lists, or cross-device profiles would be impossible without rework.

assumption_ledger.assumption: Play money only — chips/credits that exist purely within the session, never real currency and never any payment integration.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Prompt does not mention money, gambling, or payments. Real-money gambling triggers regulatory, legal, and integration burdens that are wildly out of proportion with a one-line prompt.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If the user wanted real-money play, the entire compliance, KYC, and payments stack would be missing — but more importantly, this is a hard 'do not assume' line.

assumption_ledger.assumption: Standard casino blackjack rules with sensible defaults: dealer hits on soft 17 or stands on 17 (TD picks one and documents it), blackjack pays 3:2, player can hit/stand/double-down/split, no insurance or surrender required for v1.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Prompt names the game by its common name without specifying a variant. The set above is what most people picture as 'blackjack'. Insurance and surrender are commonly omitted in casual implementations and add UI states without changing the core loop.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If a specific variant (e.g., Spanish 21, Vegas Strip rules, European no-hole-card) was intended, rule logic would need adjustment — but the core engine shape stays the same.

assumption_ledger.assumption: A standard 52-card deck, shuffled fresh for each hand (or each session). No card counting concerns, no shoe management exposed to the user.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Simplest defensible deck model. Multi-deck shoes and persistent shoe state are casino realism the prompt does not require.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: Card-counting practice tools or realistic casino simulation would need a shoe model — but those are different products.

assumption_ledger.assumption: The player starts each session with a fixed pool of play chips (e.g., 1000) and bets per hand. If they go bust they can reset.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Blackjack with no betting is just card-flipping; betting is part of what makes it the game. A fixed starting pool with reset is the simplest betting loop. Exact amount is a TD/Builder detail.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If the user wanted no betting at all (pure rules trainer), the betting UI would be unnecessary scope. If they wanted persistent bankroll across sessions, A2's no-persistence assumption blocks it.

assumption_ledger.assumption: Desktop-first responsive layout that also works on a phone in portrait, but is not designed mobile-first or as a PWA.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Prompt says 'web app' with no mention of mobile, touch, or installability. Desktop-first is the historical default for card games on the web. Basic responsiveness costs little; full mobile-first design is extra scope.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If the user wanted a phone-first, install-to-home-screen experience, viewport priorities and asset choices would need to flip.

assumption_ledger.assumption: Visual presentation is a recognizable card-table metaphor: cards rendered as cards (rank + suit), dealer area, player area, chip count, action buttons. No 3D, no animated dealer character, no voice.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Cheapest visual that reads as 'blackjack'. Anything richer is unprompted scope.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If the user wanted a stylized themed experience (e.g., neon, pixel-art, animated dealer), aesthetic direction would need a redo — but the underlying engine is unaffected.

assumption_ledger.assumption: Dealer plays automatically by fixed-rule logic (no AI, no difficulty levels). The player just plays their own hand.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Casino blackjack dealers play by deterministic rule; that is the canonical behavior. 'Difficulty' isn't a real concept in dealer play.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: Almost nothing — this is one of the most settled aspects of the game.

assumption_ledger.assumption: English-language UI only, no localization, no accessibility-beyond-basics. Standard semantic HTML and reasonable contrast are expected; full WCAG AA conformance is not a v1 goal but should not be actively undermined.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Prompt is silent on language and a11y. English + sensible defaults is the simplest baseline. Active a11y work is real engineering scope.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If a11y was a hard requirement, retrofit cost is meaningful but not catastrophic; if localization was required, copy and layout work would expand.

assumption_ledger.assumption: Single-page app with no routing, no separate screens beyond the table itself plus minor overlays (rules help, game-over modal). No settings page, no profile, no history view.
assumption_ledger.rationale: The named goal is 'play blackjack', singular. One screen accomplishes it.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: Adding views later is straightforward; this assumption mostly bounds Builder from inventing screens that weren't asked for.

assumption_ledger.assumption: No sound effects or music in v1.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Audio is unprompted, requires asset sourcing/licensing, and adds autoplay-policy and mute-control UX. Visual feedback alone can carry the game.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: Adding audio later is a scoped addition, not a rewrite.

inflection_points.topic: Betting model: fixed starting bankroll with reset, vs. no betting at all (pure rules play).
choices: Bankroll + per-hand bets (chip UI, win/lose payout, bust-and-reset)
No betting — every hand is just play, win/loss is informational only
inflection_points.why_inflection: Cascade depth is moderate (chip UI, bet selection, payout logic, game-over state all hinge on this). Reversibility is fine if caught early. UX surface is large — bet sizing is a visible interaction every hand. Option asymmetry: bankroll path is the conventional blackjack experience and what most people mean by 'play blackjack'; no-betting is genuinely simpler but arguably not the full game.

inflection_points.topic: Hand actions surface: minimal (hit / stand only) vs. full table (hit / stand / double / split, with insurance/surrender omitted).
choices: Full common actions — hit, stand, double-down, split
inflection_points.default_branch: Full common actions (hit, stand, double, split)
inflection_points.why_inflection: Cascade depth: split in particular forks hand-state into multiple parallel hands, which is real engine complexity. UX surface: visible buttons and hand layout. Reversibility: adding split later means reworking hand-state model. Option asymmetry: minimal is much cheaper to build but a player who knows blackjack will immediately notice missing actions and judge it incomplete.

out_of_scope: Real money, payments, or any gambling integration
User accounts, login, or social features
Persistent bankroll, statistics, or history across sessions or devices
AI-driven dealer, opponent personalities, or adjustable difficulty
Card-counting trainer, strategy hints, or basic-strategy coaching
Other card games (poker, baccarat, solitaire) or other blackjack variants beyond standard rules
Insurance, surrender, side bets, or progressive jackpots in v1
Native mobile app, PWA installability, or offline support
Animated 3D dealer, casino environment, or immersive theming
